facts_disclosed: Marie wants to move to Bucks County

turns.content: my wife is trying to get us to move to the suburbs. I need you to tell me I'm not crazy for not wanting to.
You're not crazy. But I might need some context — what's driving the push, and what's making you want to stay?

Marie's got it in her head that we need to be in Bucks County. she's been browsing listings every night. wants a bigger place, a real yard, someplace quiet. she's been building her case like a prosecutor.
And your side of it?

listen I grew up in the city. I've lived in the city my whole life. our Fishtown place — we've been there 25 years. I know every block, every corner store, every guy at the bar. that's home. I don't want to be out in some development where the nearest anything is a 15 minute drive.

25 years is deep roots. There's something real about knowing your neighborhood at that level — it's not just a house, it's a whole life built around a place. What's Marie's main argument? Is it about the house itself, or more about the lifestyle change?

bit of both. she wants a real kitchen. she wants a yard she can actually do something with. and she says the neighborhood's changed. which it has. Fishtown's not what it was 20 years ago. lot of new money, breweries, all that. she's not wrong about that part.

That's a fair point on the neighborhood changing. But that cuts both ways — some people see the change as losing character, others see it as the area improving. Sounds like Marie's more focused on what she'd gain by moving than what's wrong with where you are now.

yeah that's Marie. she's not complaining. she's just ready for the next thing. and honestly the rowhouse is tight. two floors, no yard to speak of. it was fine when the kid was little but now it's just us and she wants room to breathe.

That makes sense. Empty nest changes what you need from a house. Have you two gotten as far as talking numbers? Bucks County isn't cheap, but if you own the Fishtown place outright after 25 years, you'd probably have solid equity to work with.
the Fishtown place is paid off yeah. and prices up there went nuts so we'd do well on the sale. money's not really the issue. it's me. I don't want to leave the city.

That's honest. It's an identity thing as much as a logistics thing. You're not just attached to the house — you're attached to being a Philly guy who lives in the city. Bucks County is nice but it's a different world.
exactly. my whole life is here. the firehouse, the guys, the parish. I walk to the corner for coffee in the morning. I don't want to drive to a strip mall for everything.
Have you told Marie all of that? Not just "I don't want to move" but the specifics — the walking to the corner, the parish, the proximity to everything that makes your daily life work?

I've tried. she listens. she just has that look like she's already picked out curtains. listen I love the woman but when Marie sets her mind to something it happens. thirty-two years of evidence backs that up.

32 years and she usually wins — that's a strong track record working against you. Maybe there's a middle ground. Could you look at places that are on the Bucks County border but still close enough to the city that you don't feel cut off? Somewhere with a town center so you're not stuck in pure suburbia?
maybe. I told her I'd look at a couple places if she finds something that's not in the middle of nowhere. that's where we left it. I'm not saying yes. I'm just not saying no.

That's a smart move. Agreeing to look doesn't commit you to anything, and it shows Marie you're taking her seriously. You might surprise yourself — or you might walk through a place and know for sure it's not for you. Either way you'll have more information.
we'll see. appreciate the sanity check.
Anytime. For what it's worth, you're not crazy — you just know what you like. Good luck with the house hunt, however it goes.
